The essays by Miller and Straus give complete bibliographic references on first mention in their notes
I have maintained this apparatus here and followed it in my own essays
Bethea's essay uses shorthand references to the "Works Cited" section in his chapter (excluding references to well-known literary works)
Because each essay has its own set of references, there is inevitably overlap between the individual studies and the final Select Bibliography
At the same time, the individual contributions contain some references that do not appear in the Select Bibliography, which lists only studies treating The Idiot
All references to Dostoevsky's works and letters in this volume are to the Polnoe sobranie sochinenii v tridsati tomakh, ed
(Leningrad: Nauka, 1972-90)
The reference "8:123," for example, refers to page 123 of volume 8
Volumes 28-30, which contain Dostoevsky's letters, are (excluding references to well-known literary works)
Because each essay has its own set of references, there is inevitably overlap between the individual studies and the final Select Bibliography
At the same time, the individual contributions contain some references that do not appear in the Select Bibliography, which lists only studies treating The Idiot
All references to Dostoevsky's works and letters in this volume are to the Polnoe sobranie sochinenii v tridsati tomakh, ed
(Leningrad: Nauka, 1972-90)
The reference "8:123," for example, refers to page 123 of volume 8
Volumes 28-30, which contain Dostoevsky's letters, are each divided into two books
2:136" refers to page 136 of book 2 of volume 28
The dates of Dostoevsky's letters are also included in the citations
It might help to recall that the Russian calendar at this point lagged behind the Western calendar by twelve days
When two dates are given (for example, July 9/21), the first is the Russian date, the second a translation of that date into the Western calendar
The Select Bibliography uses the Library of Congress transliteration system
My contributions use a more popular mode of rendering Russian names
In the reprinted essays, the usage of the author has been maintained
Translations of letters included in the Primary Sources are those of David Lowe
Translations of other material in the Primary Sources are my own
I INTRODUCTION Introduction to The Idiot Part 1 : Where, When, and How The Idiot Was Written LIZA KNAPP 1
Escape from Petersburg On Holy Friday 1867, Fyodor Dostoevsky and his young wife, Anna, left Russia for what they thought would be a few months
They did not return until July 1871
In setting off for Europe, Dostoevsky left behind him in Saint Petersburg heavy debts and complicated family obligations
